subject: Physics
question_text: When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why? When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave? In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
question_html:
1. When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why?
  2. When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave?
  3. In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
solution_text: Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.
solution_html:
1. Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light.
  2. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation.
  3. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.

subject: Physics
question_text: Discuss the intensity of transmitted light when a polaroid sheet is rotated between two crossed polaroids?
question_html: Discuss the intensity of transmitted light when a polaroid sheet is rotated between two crossed polaroids?
solution_text: Let I 0 be the intensity of polarised light after passing through the first polariser P 1 . Then the intensity of light after passing through second polariser P 2 will be {tex}I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta{/tex} where {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between axes of P 1 and P 2 . Since P 1 and P 3 are crossed the angle between the pass axes of P 2 and P 3 will be {tex}I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \sin ^{2} \theta= I_0(2sin\theta cos\theta)^2/4=\left(I_{0} / 4\right) \sin ^{2} 2 \theta{/tex} Therefore, the transmitted intensity will be maximum sin {tex}2\theta{/tex} = 1 hence 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 0 hence {tex}\theta = \frac {\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I0 be the intensity of polarised light after passing through the first polariser P1. Then the intensity of light after passing through second polariser P2 will be
{tex}I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta{/tex}
where {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between axes of P1 and P2. Since P1 and P3 are crossed the angle between the pass axes of P2 and P3 will be

{tex}I=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex}
{tex}=I_{0} \cos ^{2} \theta \sin ^{2} \theta= I_0(2sin\theta cos\theta)^2/4=\left(I_{0} / 4\right) \sin ^{2} 2 \theta{/tex}
Therefore, the transmitted intensity will be maximum sin{tex}2\theta{/tex} = 1 hence  2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 900    hence  {tex}\theta = \frac {\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
solution_text: Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex} {tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex} For reflected light, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, and 589 nm respectively. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air. {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex} Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex} Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex}
{tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex}

  1. For reflected light,
    Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
    {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}
    Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz, and 589 nm respectively.
  2. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air.
    {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex}
    Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation,
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex}
    {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex}
    Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.

subject: Physics
question_text: What is the shape of the wavefront in each of the following cases: Light diverging from a point source. Light emerging out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus. The portion of the wavefront of light from a distant star intercepted by the Earth.
question_html:
What is the shape of the wavefront in each of the following cases:

  1. Light diverging from a point source.
  2. Light emerging out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.
  3. The portion of the wavefront of light from a distant star intercepted by the Earth.
solution_text: Spherical Plane Plane (Because a small area on the surface of a large sphere is nearly planar)
solution_html:
1. Spherical
  2. Plane
  3. Plane (Because a small area on the surface of a large sphere is nearly planar)

subject: Physics
question_text: The refractive index of glass is 1.5. What is the speed of light in glass? (Speed of light in vacuum is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m s -1 ) Is the speed of light in glass independent of the colour of light? If not, which of the two colours red and violet travels slower in a glass prism?
question_html:
1. The refractive index of glass is 1.5. What is the speed of light in glass? (Speed of light in vacuum is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m s-1)
  2. Is the speed of light in glass independent of the colour of light? If not, which of the two colours red and violet travels slower in a glass prism?
solution_text: Refractive index of glass {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 . So, Speed of light in glass is v= {tex}\frac {c}{\mu}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s The speed of light in glass is not independent of the colour of light. The refractive index of a violet component of white light is greater than the refractive index of a red component. Hence, the speed of violet light is less than the speed of red light in the glass. Hence, violet light travels slower than red light in a glass prism.
solution_html:
1. Refractive index of glass {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 . So, Speed of light in glass is v= {tex}\frac {c}{\mu}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}108 m/s
  2. The speed of light in glass is not independent of the colour of light. The refractive index of a violet component of white light is greater than the refractive index of a red component. Hence, the speed of violet light is less than the speed of red light in the glass. Hence, violet light travels slower than red light in a glass prism.

subject: Physics
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.28 mm and the screen is placed 1.4 m away. The distance between the central bright fringe and fourth bright fringe is measured to be 1.2 cm. Determine the wavelength of light used in the experiment.
question_html: In Young's double-slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.28 mm and the screen is placed 1.4 m away. The distance between the central bright fringe and fourth bright fringe is measured to be 1.2 cm. Determine the wavelength of light used in the experiment.
solution_text: Given, d = 0.28 mm = 0.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1.4 m, y = 1.2 cm = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m, n = 4, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = ? For constructive interference, distance between two fringes, y = {tex}\frac{{nD\lambda }}{d}{/tex} Thus, wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{yd}}{{nD}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 0.28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{4 \times 1.4}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm
solution_html:
Given, d = 0.28 mm = 0.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, D = 1.4 m, y = 1.2 cm = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m, n = 4, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = ?
For constructive interference, distance between two fringes, y = {tex}\frac{{nD\lambda }}{d}{/tex}
Thus, wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{yd}}{{nD}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 0.28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{4 \times 1.4}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm

subject: Physics
question_text: In Young’s double slit experiment using monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , the intensity of light at a point on the screen where path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex} , is K units. Find out the intensity of light at a point, where path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} .
question_html: In Young’s double slit experiment using monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex}, is K units. Find out the intensity of light at a point, where path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}.
solution_text: The intensity of light is given by I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} When path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex} : A path difference of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is equivalent to a phase difference of 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4 I 0 cos 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 4 I 0 (-1) 2 = 4 I 0 = K When path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} : A path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} is equivalent to a phase difference of {tex}\frac{2\pi}{3}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} I' = 4 I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 4 I 0 ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) 2 = I 0 = {tex}\frac{K}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
The intensity of light is given by
I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}

  1. When path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex} : A path difference of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is equivalent to a phase difference of 2{tex}\pi{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4 I0 cos2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 4 I0 (-1)2 = 4 I0 = K
  2. When path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} : A path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} is equivalent to a phase difference of {tex}\frac{2\pi}{3}{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I' = 4 I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 4 I0 ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex})2 = I0 = {tex}\frac{K}{4}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths, 650 nm and 520 nm, are used to obtain interference fringes in a Young's double slit experiment. Find the distance of the third bright fringe on the screen from the central maximum for wavelength 650 nm. What is the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide?
question_html:
A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths, 650 nm and 520 nm, are used to obtain interference fringes in a Young's double slit experiment.

  1. Find the distance of the third bright fringe on the screen from the central maximum for wavelength 650 nm.
  2. What is the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide?
solution_text: Here, {tex}{\lambda _1} = 650 \ nm = 650 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}{\lambda _2} = 520\;nm = 520 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} Suppose, d = distance between two slits D = Distance of screen from the slits For third bright fringe, n = 3 {tex}x = n\;{\lambda _1} \cdot \frac{D}{d}{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 650 \times \frac{{D}}{{d}} =1950 \frac{{D}}{{d}}{/tex} Let nth bright fringe due to wavelength 650 nm coincide with (n -1)th due to wavelength 520 nm. Therefore, {tex}n\;{\lambda _2} = \left( {n - 1} \right){\lambda _1}{/tex} or, {tex}n \times 520 = \left( {n - 1} \right) \times 650\Rightarrow n=5{/tex} Hence, the least distance from the central maximum can be obtained by the relation: {tex}x = nλ_2 \frac{D}{d} = 5 \times 520 \frac{D}{d} = 2600 \frac{D}{d}\;nm{/tex} Note: The value of d and D are not given in the question.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}{\lambda _1} = 650 \ nm = 650 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
{tex}{\lambda _2} = 520\;nm = 520 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
Suppose, d = distance between two slits
D = Distance of screen from the slits

  1. For third bright fringe, n = 3
    {tex}x = n\;{\lambda _1} \cdot \frac{D}{d}{/tex}
    {tex} = 3 \times 650 \times \frac{{D}}{{d}} =1950 \frac{{D}}{{d}}{/tex}
  2. Let nth bright fringe due to wavelength 650 nm coincide with (n -1)th due to wavelength 520 nm.
    Therefore,  {tex}n\;{\lambda _2} = \left( {n - 1} \right){\lambda _1}{/tex}
    or, {tex}n \times 520 = \left( {n - 1} \right) \times 650\Rightarrow n=5{/tex}
    Hence, the least distance from the central maximum can be obtained by the relation:
    {tex}x = nλ_2 \frac{D}{d} = 5 \times 520 \frac{D}{d} = 2600 \frac{D}{d}\;nm{/tex}
    Note: The value of d and D are not given in the question.
